candidate_problems.text: How can 'system-level persistence' be defined and measured non-arbitrarily so it is distinguishable from ordinary far-from-equilibrium chemistry? The conjecture hinges on a boundary between generic dissipative structures and genuinely life-like closure of productive loops, but it does not yet specify operational criteria for when a network is reproducing a causal organization rather than merely persisting transiently under external forcing.

What mechanism could drive the transition from distributed, environmentally scaffolded chemical memory to portable template-based inheritance without presupposing already-Darwinian replicators? The conjecture proposes that templates were a later compression and stabilization of network-level organization, but the selective pathway, intermediate forms, and transfer of 'knowledge' from topology/spatial structure into sequences remain unresolved.

Can network-level autocatalytic organizations achieve sufficient heredity, variation, and differential persistence to support cumulative open-ended evolution before templates exist? If not, the conjecture may explain local persistence but not the growth of biological complexity; if yes, it raises the problem of identifying the minimal structural conditions under which distributed chemical systems become evolvable rather than merely self-maintaining.
